title: If in a triangle two angles be equal to one another, the sides which subtend the equal angles will also be equal to one another.

steps.text: Let ABC be a triangle having the angle ABC equal to the angle ACB.
For, if AB were unequal to AC, one of them would be greater. Suppose AB greater; from AB the greater let DB be cut off equal to AC the less (Prop. I.3).
Then, since DB would equal AC, and BC is common, and the angle DBC equals the angle ACB, triangle DBC would equal triangle ACB (Prop. I.4) — the less equals the greater: which is absurd.

Therefore AB is not unequal to AC; it is therefore equal to it. Wherefore if in a triangle two angles be equal to one another, the sides which subtend the equal angles are equal to one another, as was required.
